
<br> 
<center><img src="https://i.imgur.com/AbCCpQO.png" width="700"></center>


_____

# Spatial data and Interactive maps 


```{r}
rm(list = ls()) # start fresh

library(sf)
contrib_zipMap=st_read("https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/main/contrib_polygons_light.gpkg")
wa_simple <- read_sf('https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/main/maps/WA_State_Boundary.geojson')
```

```{r}
names(contrib_zipMap)
```

Interactive maps are useful when readers need to zoom in and explore the map directly in the browser. Here we use the `leaflet` package. 

# A simple interactive map

Leaflet works like this:
```{r}
library(leaflet)

leafletObject<- leaflet() %>% # create the leaflet object
                  addTiles() # add the background
leafletObject
```


Given we have the `wa_simple` object, we simply **add** its polygons:

```{r}
leafletObject%>% 
  addPolygons(
    data = wa_simple)
```

Let's add some details to create our `baseMap`:
```{r}
baseMap<-leafletObject%>% 
  addPolygons(
    data = wa_simple,
    fillColor = "white", #polygon(s) fill color
    weight = 0,    # border width
    fillOpacity = 1) # 0 is totally trasparent, 1 full color

baseMap

```



# A simple choropleth


We begin with one example: Republican contributions in 2012.

### 1. Subset to create `map_Rep2012`:

```{r}
map_Rep2012 <- contrib_zipMap[
  contrib_zipMap$election_year == 2012 &
  contrib_zipMap$party == "REPUBLICAN", ]
```

### 2. Create a color palette for **share_contributions_cat** using `colorFactor()`:

```{r, message=FALSE}

paletteFun <- colorFactor(
                  palette = "YlOrRd",
                  domain = map_Rep2012$share_contributions_cat
              )
```

### 3. Commonly, we create labels for hover text

```{r}
popUp_labels <- sprintf(
                  "<strong>%s</strong>",
                  map_Rep2012$ZCTA5CE10
                ) |> lapply(htmltools::HTML)
```

### 4. First draft, adding the polygons from `map_Rep2012`

```{r}
Rep2012 <- baseMap %>%
  addPolygons(
    data = map_Rep2012
  )

Rep2012
```


### 5. Now add the fill color

```{r}
Rep2012 <- baseMap %>%
  addPolygons(
    data = map_Rep2012,
    fillColor = ~paletteFun(share_contributions_cat)
  )

Rep2012
```

### 6. Final Touches

* **Retouching the polygons:**

```{r}
Rep2012 <- baseMap %>%
  addPolygons(
    data = map_Rep2012,
    fillColor = ~paletteFun(share_contributions_cat),
    stroke = FALSE,# no border
    fillOpacity = 0.8, # improve light
    label = popUp_labels, #text when hovering
    ## optional
    labelOptions = labelOptions(
      textsize = "15px") # change size of text
  )

Rep2012

```


* **Adding a legend**

```{r}
Rep2012 %>%
  addLegend(
    data = map_Rep2012,
    values = ~share_contributions_cat,
    pal = paletteFun,
    title = "Contributions",
    position = "bottomright"
  )
```


# Interacting layers with legend 

Now we create a map where each contribution category is a separate layer. This allows users to turn categories on and off.

We use Democratic contributions in 2012. Remember we have these levels:

```{r}
theLabelsForLevels <- sort(unique(contrib_zipMap$share_contributions_cat))
theLabelsForLevels
```

Let's subset to keep democrats in 2012

```{r}
contribWA2012_demo <- contrib_zipMap[
  contrib_zipMap$election_year == 2012 &
  contrib_zipMap$party == "DEMOCRAT", ]
```


Different than before, we will write the color manually. Let's use a color safe [palette] (https://colorbrewer2.org/). Here I will use the colors of the diverging palette "PuOr" for 4 levels (I will copy and paste them into a vector). Notice I will use _transparency_('fillOpacity') for the most common levels:

```{r}
# the colors
PuOr <- c("#e66101", "#fdb863", "#b2abd2", "#5e3c99")

# the opacity for each
theOpacity <- c(1, 0.5, 0.5, 1)
```


Let's take advantage of the `split()` function:


```{r}
demo_splitted <- split(x = contribWA2012_demo,
                       f = contribWA2012_demo$share_contributions_cat)

```


`demo_splitted` is a list of 4 dataframes, one for each category in **share_contributions_cat**

```{r}
length(demo_splitted)
```

Take a look at this *old-fashioned* for loop:
```{r}

for (i in 1:length(demo_splitted)) {
      print(dim(demo_splitted[[i]])) # the dataframe i's dimensions
      print(PuOr[i]) # the dataframe i's color
      print(theOpacity[i]) # the dataframe i's opacity
      print(names(demo_splitted)[i]) # the dataframe i's group or category

  }
```

As you see, you can visit those important objects in parallel. Let's use that to build the FOUR leaflet maps, and plot one on top of the other:

```{r}
map2012_demo <- baseMap

for (i in 1:length(demo_splitted)) {
      map2012_demo <- map2012_demo %>%
                        addPolygons(
                          stroke = FALSE,
                          data = demo_splitted[[i]],
                          color = PuOr[i],
                          fillOpacity = theOpacity[i],
                          # this is the most important!!
                          group = names(demo_splitted)[i]
                        )}
map2012_demo  
```

ABove you see o difference, but here you will the effect of `group = names(demo_splitted)[i]`
```{r}
map2012_demo <- map2012_demo %>% 
                  addLayersControl(
                    overlayGroups = theLabelsForLevels,
                    options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE))

map2012_demo 
```


Notice that if you start zooming in, it might be difficult to go back to a whole view of our main map. Leaflet does not give you a **home** button by default. This code helps you with that:
```{r}
textFun <- "function(btn, map){map.setView([47.751076, -120.740135], 7)}"

finalZoom <- map2012_demo %>%
  addEasyButton(
    easyButton(
      icon = "fa-home",
      title = "Zoom to Washington",
      onClick = JS(textFun)
    )
  )
finalZoom
```

## Facetting

Facetting is not straightforward. If we use the previous map, we can build another one for democrats:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
# creating sub maps
contribWA2016=contrib_zipMap[contrib_zipMap$election_year==2016,]
contribWA2016_demo=contribWA2016[contribWA2016$party=='DEMOCRAT',]

map10b=contribWA2016_demo[contribWA2016_demo$share_contributions_cat==theLabelsForLevels[1],]
map100b=contribWA2016_demo[contribWA2016_demo$share_contributions_cat==theLabelsForLevels[2],]
map1000b=contribWA2016_demo[contribWA2016_demo$share_contributions_cat==theLabelsForLevels[3],]
map10000b=contribWA2016_demo[contribWA2016_demo$share_contributions_cat==theLabelsForLevels[4],]

# plotting

layer1b= leaflet() %>%
            addTiles() %>%
        addPolygons(data=map10b,
                    color=PuOr[1],fillOpacity = 1,stroke = F,
                    group = theLabelsForLevels[1]) # LAYER as GROUP

layer1_2b= layer1%>%addPolygons(data=map100b,
                               color=PuOr[2],fillOpacity = 0.5,stroke = F,
                               group = theLabelsForLevels[2])


layer1_2_3b= layer1_2%>%addPolygons(data=map1000b,
                                   color=PuOr[3],fillOpacity = 0.5,stroke = F,
                                   group = theLabelsForLevels[3])

layer1_2_3_4b= layer1_2_3%>%addPolygons(data=map10000b,
                                   color=PuOr[4],fillOpacity = 1,stroke = F,
                                   group = theLabelsForLevels[4])

textFun="function(btn, map){map.setView([47.751076, -120.740135], 7)}"

# Let's not request the HOME button..

finalLayersb=layer1_2_3_4b %>% addLayersControl(
        overlayGroups = theLabelsForLevels,
        options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE))
```

Does this help? You need *leafsync* library:

```{r}
library(leafsync)
finalFacet=sync(finalZoomLayers,finalLayersb,ncol = 2)
finalFacet
```
